text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST — PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPHASIC)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Raymond Kowalski, 66-year-old male with biopsy-proven borderline resectable pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (cT3N1M0, Stage III). Currently undergoing neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy; this examination is performed for mid-treatment restaging after completion of cycle 4 (approximately 8 weeks of therapy). Baseline CA 19-9 was 1200 U/mL; most recent CA 19-9 reported at 380 U/mL. Comparison: Prior pancreatic protocol CT performed at initial staging.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed in the non-contrast, arterial (pancreatic parenchymal), portal venous, and delayed phases following administration of 100 mL of nonionic intravenous contrast (iohexol 350 mg I/mL) at a rate of 4 mL/second. Oral contrast was not administered. Axial, coronal, and sagittal reconstructions reviewed with dedicated pancreatic windowing. Curvilinear and maximum intensity projection reconstructions were reviewed for vascular assessment.

---

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
There is a persistent ill-defined, hypoenhancing mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas. On the current examination, the mass measures approximately 3.1 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 3.8 cm on the baseline staging CT), representing a meaningful decrease in overall size. The margins of the mass remain irregular and poorly circumscribed, blending with the surrounding desmoplastic stroma, though there appears to be a subtle decrease in the degree of perilesional soft tissue stranding and associated mesenteric haziness in the immediate vicinity of the mass when compared to the baseline examination. A small central area of low attenuation within the lesion may represent early necrosis or treatment-related change, though this finding is nonspecific. The degree of upstream main pancreatic ductal dilatation appears marginally decreased, now measuring approximately 5 mm in maximal diameter (previously 6 mm), suggesting partial relief of ductal obstruction. A biliary stent is in place within the distal common bile duct; the upstream intrahepatic bile ducts and common hepatic duct appear decompressed and within normal caliber, consistent with adequate biliary drainage.

VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS:
On the prior examination, the pancreatic head mass was noted to abut the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 120–150 degrees of its circumference. On the current study, the degree of soft tissue contact along the right lateral and posterior aspect of the SMA appears reduced. The fat plane between the leading edge of the mass and the SMA is still not completely restored, but the extent of circumferential soft tissue contact now appears to involve less than 90 degrees of the arterial wall. No teardrop deformity or luminal irregularity of the SMA is identified. There is no evidence of arterial encasement.

The superior mesenteric vein–portal vein (SMV-PV) confluence continues to demonstrate short-segment contact with the mass along its posterior aspect. The degree of this contact appears roughly stable compared to the baseline study. The venous lumen remains patent without evidence of occlusion, thrombosis, or definitive encasement. No teardrop deformity of the SMV is appreciated. The confluence appears widely patent on the portal venous phase images.

The celiac axis and its major branches (splenic artery, common hepatic artery, left gastric artery) are patent and without evidence of involvement by tumor. The hepatic artery proper, right hepatic artery, and left hepatic artery arise in typical fashion and appear uninvolved.

LYMPH NODES:
Two previously identified periportal/hepatoduodenal ligament lymph nodes appear decreased in overall size. The largest node in this region now measures approximately 0.8 cm in short-axis dimension (previously up to 1.2 cm). The nodes are no longer as conspicuous and appear borderline in size. No new pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the celiac, peripancreatic, retroperitoneal, or mesenteric distributions.

LIVER:
The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous enhancement in all phases. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No hypervascular or hypovascular hepatic masses. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein is patent. Hepatic veins and inferior vena cava are patent.

GALLBLADDER AND BILIARY SYSTEM:
A previously placed plastic biliary stent is again visualized traversing the distal common bile duct. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable without wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or gallstones. The intrahepatic ducts appear decompressed.

SPLEEN:
The spleen is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without focal lesions. No splenomegaly. Splenic vein is patent.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear within normal limits with no focal nodules or masses beyond expected adrenal limb width.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS:
Bilateral kidneys are symmetric in size and demonstrate normal cortical thickness and enhancement. No renal masses, hydronephrosis, or nephrolithiasis. No ureteral abnormality.

STOMACH AND SMALL BOWEL:
The stomach is unremarkable. The duodenum is partially obscured by the adjacent pancreatic head mass, as expected; no upstream gastric or duodenal obstruction is appreciated. No small bowel obstruction or abnormal mural thickening. No mesenteric edema.

COLON:
The large bowel is not distended and shows no evidence of wall thickening or mass. Appendix is visualized and within normal limits.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
No peritoneal nodules, omental thickening, or soft tissue implants to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. No free fluid. No ascites. Mesenteric fat stranding is mild and appears decreased compared to the prior examination, likely related to treatment response.

RETROPERITONEUM:
The aorta is mildly ectatic in the infrarenal position (2.4 cm), unchanged and not aneurysmal. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No retroperitoneal mass or hematoma.

BONY STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES:
The visualized osseous structures are intact without lytic or blastic lesions to suggest osseous metastases. Mild multilevel degenerative changes are noted in the lumbar spine. Abdominal wall is intact without hernia or mass. Subcutaneous soft tissues are unremarkable.

PELVIS:
Bladder is well distended and unremarkable. Prostate gland is mildly enlarged, consistent with benign prostatic hypertrophy. No pelvic free fluid. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. Iliac vessels are patent.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. PANCREATIC HEAD MASS — DECREASED IN SIZE: The hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process has decreased from 3.8 cm to approximately 3.1 cm since the baseline staging examination, consistent with a meaningful morphologic response to neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy. Accompanying reduction in perilesional soft tissue stranding and slight improvement in upstream pancreatic ductal dilatation further support treatment effect.

2. IMPROVED VASCULAR ABUTMENT: The degree of soft tissue contact between the primary mass and the superior mesenteric artery appears diminished, now estimated at less than 90 degrees of circumference compared to 120–150 degrees at baseline. No arterial encasement. The SMA fat plane shows early signs of partial reconstitution, though complete clearance has not been achieved.

3. SMV-PV INTERFACE — STABLE WITHOUT OCCLUSION: Short-segment contact of the tumor with the SMV-portal vein confluence is again noted, appearing stable in extent without new venous compromise. The venous confluence remains widely patent.

4. INTERVAL DECREASE IN PERIPORTAL LYMPH NODES: The previously suspicious periportal lymph nodes have decreased in size, now measuring up to 0.8 cm (previously 1.2 cm). These nodes remain borderline and warrant continued monitoring, but the trend is encouraging.

5. NO DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE: No hepatic lesions, no peritoneal implants, no ascites, and no new lymphadenopathy suspicious for distant spread. No evidence of hepatic or pulmonary metastases on the current study.

6. BILIARY STENT IN PLACE WITH ADEQUATE DECOMPRESSION: The distal common bile duct stent remains appropriately positioned with decompressed biliary system.

Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with a favorable response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy with a reduction in primary tumor bulk, improved vascular anatomy relative to the SMA, and decrease in regional lymph node conspicuity. The constellation of findings suggests the tumor may be trending toward a more favorable surgical posture, though continued neoadjuvant therapy and repeat restaging after additional cycles would be appropriate to further characterize the degree of response and reassess resectability.
